coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase by switching on gold nanoclusters fluorescence quenched by pyridoxal phosphate
coredata.dc:creator: Halawa, Mohamed Ibrahim
Gao, Wenyue
Saqib, Muhammad
Kitte, Shimeles Addisu
Wu, Fengxia
Xu, Guobao
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we designed highly sensitive and selective luminescent detection method for alkaline phosphatase using bovine serum albumin functionalized gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) as the nanosensor probe and pyridoxal phosphate as the substrate of alkaline phosphatase. We found that pyridoxal phosphate can quench the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs and pyridoxal has little effect on the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs. The proposed mechanism of fluorescence quenching by PLP was explored on the basis of data obtained from high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV–vis spectrophotometry, fluorescence spectroscopy, fluorescence decay time measurements and circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. Alkaline phosphatase catalyzes the hydrolysis of pyridoxal phosphate to generate pyridoxal, restoring the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs. Therefore, a recovery type approach has been developed for the sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase in the range of 1.0–200.0U/L (R2 =0.995) with a detection limit of 0.05U/L. The proposed sensor exhibit excellent selectivity among various enzymes, such as glucose oxidase, lysozyme, trypsin, papain, and pepsin. The present switch-on fluorescence sensing strategy for alkaline phosphatase was successfully applied in human serum plasma with good recoveries (100.60–104.46%), revealing that this nanosensor probe is a promising tool for ALP detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bovine serum albumin protected gold nanoclusters
Pyridoxal phosphate
Alkaline phosphatase
Pyridoxal
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure of the determination of PLP
Procedure of the determination of ALP activity
Sensing ALP activity in human serum samples
Quenching of BSA-AuNCs fluorescence by PLP
Application of the developed method for the detection of ALP activity
Selectivity of the PLP quenched BSA-AuNCs for ALP assay
ALP sensing in diluted human serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate the kind support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475123 and 21505128), the Ministry of Science and technology of the People's Republic of China (No. 2016YFA0201300), and the Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS)–the Academy of Sciences for the Developing World (TWAS) President's Fellowship Program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV spectra of BSA-AuNCs (a), excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of BSA-AuNCs.

Fluorescence spectra of BSA-AuNCs (black line) in the presence of 1.0mM PLP (blue line), PL (red line) and fluorescence spectrum of 1.0mM PLP only (green line). Excitation wavelength: 370nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra of BSA-AuNCs (black line) in the presence of different concentrations of PLP in 0.01M phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). B) The calibration curve between the quenching in fluorescence (F0/F) and concentrations of PLP.

Luminescence decay curves of BSA-AuNCs (green line) and BSA-AuNCs in presence of 100.0µM PLP (red line). Excitation wavelength: 377nm, emission wavelength: 626nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
A) Fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs in the presence of different activities of ALP. (B) The calibration curve between the recovered fluorescence intensity (F–F0) and ALP activities.

Selectivity of the proposed method for ALP detection. The concentrations are 10μg/L for GOx, lysozyme, trypsin, papain, or pepsin; 1.0mM for GSH and cysteine; 5.0mM (not including ion concentration from the original buffer system) for Mg2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, and SO4 2−. F0 was the fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs quenched by PLP and F was the recovery fluorescence intensity in the presence of 50U/L ALP or other different substance.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase by switching on gold nanoclusters fluorescence quenched by pyridoxal phosphate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Mansoura, 35516 Mansoura, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Mansoura

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we designed highly sensitive and selective luminescent detection method for alkaline phosphatase using bovine serum albumin functionalized gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) as the nanosensor probe and pyridoxal phosphate as the substrate of alkaline phosphatase. We found that pyridoxal phosphate can quench the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs and pyridoxal has little effect on the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs. The proposed mechanism of fluorescence quenching by PLP was explored on the basis of data obtained from high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV–vis spectrophotometry, fluorescence spectroscopy, fluorescence decay time measurements and circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. Alkaline phosphatase catalyzes the hydrolysis of pyridoxal phosphate to generate pyridoxal, restoring the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs. Therefore, a recovery type approach has been developed for the sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase in the range of 1.0–200.0U/L (R2 =0.995) with a detection limit of 0.05U/L. The proposed sensor exhibit excellent selectivity among various enzymes, such as glucose oxidase, lysozyme, trypsin, papain, and pepsin. The present switch-on fluorescence sensing strategy for alkaline phosphatase was successfully applied in human serum plasma with good recoveries (100.60–104.46%), revealing that this nanosensor probe is a promising tool for ALP detection.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Bovine serum albumin protected gold nanoclusters

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) plays a crucial role in biological systems and is one of the most vastly assayed enzymes. It catalyzes the hydrolysis of wide variety of monophosphate esters substrates, such as 5-bromo- 4-chloro-3-indolyl phosphate (BCIP), p-nitrophenylphosphate (pNPP), ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP). Detection of ALP is of great paramount importance in diagnostics as the abnormal serum level of ALP is closely affined to many diseases, such as ovarian, breast, prostatic cancers as well as osteoblastic bone cancer and liver tumors (Fernandez and Kidney, 2007). Also ALP is diagnostic biomarker for other diseases, such as diabetes, heart failure, Paget's disease and obstructive jaundice (Colombatto et al., 1996; Couttenye et al., 1996). It is frequently used in ELISA, (Jin et al., 2015) histochemical assay (Unger et al., 2007) and western blotting as important labeling enzyme in clinical experiments (Sakisaka et al., 2015).

Recently, various strategies have been reported for the sensitive detection of ALP. Many of these methods are based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (Li et al., 2016b; Qian et al., 2015a, 2015b; Tang et al., 2016), inorganic or organic fluorophores (Deng et al., 2015; Li et al., 2016a), and nanomaterial based signal amplification strategies or colorimetric methods (Dong et al., 2015; Hayat and Andreescu, 2013; Kang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2015b, 2015c). Even though these approaches can detect sensitively and selectively the activity of ALP, these approaches have some drawbacks including small stokes shift, poor photostability and complicated synthesis process.

Noble metal nanoclusters acquired great interest last decade due to its intriguing characters. Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) consist of tens of Au atoms with sizes equal to the Fermi wavelength of electrons characterized by discrete energy levels. The fluorescence of AuNCs highly depends on their sizes, structures, surface ligands, pH, temperature and ionic strength of the surrounding medium. Recently, the bovine serum albumin protected gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) have significant interest in biological labeling, imaging, and biological applications (Archana et al., 2010; Chang and Ho, 2015; Hu et al., 2016; Tao et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2015a) due to their unique features such as high fluorescence, good water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, good photostability, and large stokes shift (Choi et al., 2012; Lu and Chen, 2012; Shang et al., 2011).

Herein, we report a facile, sensitive and highly selective method for probing the activity of ALP based on the “switch-on” of BSA-AuNCs fluorescence. We presented pyridoxal phosphate (PLP) as a new substrate in biological sensing for ALP through modulating BSA-AuNCs fluorescence. The fluorescence of the BSA-AuNCs was initially quenched by PLP and then restored after the addition of ALP due to the enzymatic hydrolysis of PLP to pyridoxal (PL) that is not able to quench BSA-AuNCs fluorescence. PLP was selected as ALP substrate for following reasons. (a) It is cheap, water soluble and biocompatible. (b) Its fluorescence emission peak is far away from BSA-AuNCs emission peak. (c) It acts as coenzyme for a variety of important biological reactions such as homocysteine metabolism, transamination and decarboxylation reactions; therefore this proposed method can open up a lot of prospect biological applications.

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), bovine serum albumin (BSA), glucose oxidase (GOx), trypsin, and lysozyme were purchased from Sigma. Pepsin and papain were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate monohydrate (PLP) was purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), pyridoxal·HCl (PL), glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.. Other chemicals used were of analytical grade.

All fluorescence measurements were carried on a RF-5301PC spectrophotofluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan), equipped with a 150Watt Xenon arc lamp. The excitation and emission wavelengths were 370nm and 611nm, respectively. The slit widths were 5nm for excitation spectra and 10nm for emission spectra, Quartz 0.1cm cuvette was used for all measurements. Unico UV–visible double beam spectrophotometer (LS10-0179, Changchun, China) with 10.0mm path-length quartz cuvette was used to measure the absorbance. The hydrodynamic sizes of the AuNCs were determined by dynamic light scattering analysis using a Zetasizer nano ZS90, particle size and zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Instruments, U.K.). Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were measured using a JASCO J-820 spectropolarimeter (Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a Peltier temperature controller. The Fluorescence lifetimes were measured on spectrofluorimeter with the model of FLS920 from Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, UK, equipped with EPL375 pulsed laser diode. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were performed using a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 electron microscope operated at 200kV.

BSA-AuNCs were synthesized according to the previous strategy by Xie and co-authors (Xie et al., 2009). All glassware was rinsed thoroughly with freshly prepared Aqua Regia (HCl: HNO3; 3:1v/v) and washed with ultrapure bidistilled water. In a typical experiment, 10.0mM aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (5.0mL, 37°C) was added to 5.0mL of BSA aqueous solution (50.0mg/mL, 37°C) under vigorous stirring at 37°C. After about two minutes, NaOH solution (0.5mL, 1mM) was added and the reaction mixture was incubated at 37°C for 12h. The color of the reaction mixture changed from light yellow to light brown and then became deep brown as the reaction proceeded. The final synthesized solution of BSA-AuNCs was kept at 4°C prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of the determination of PLP
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of PLP, 100.0μL aliquots solution of BSA-AuNCs were added to phosphate buffer solution (10mM, 300μL, pH=7.5), then a series of 100.0μL PLP solution of various concentrations (50.0 μmol/ L to 1.0mmol/L) was introduced and mixed thoroughly to interact with BSA-AuNCs. The fluorescence spectra were then measured (excitation 370nm; maximum emission 611nm) after 5min. The excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5.0 and 10.0nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of the determination of ALP activity
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 100.0μL of PLP (5.0mmol/L), 10.0μL of different concentrations of ALP in carbonate buffer solution (90.0μL, 0.01M, pH=10) containing 1.0µM MgSO4 and 0.1µM ZnCl2 were mixed and incubated for 30min at 37°C. The resulting solution was then added to 100.0μL of the synthesized BSA-AuNCs and diluted with phosphate buffer solution (200μL, 10mM) so as to the final concentration of PLP in the reaction mixture was 1.0mM and its pH was 7.5. The fluorescence emission spectra of the system were recorded after excitation at 370nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing ALP activity in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Blood samples were collected from human healthy volunteer from Jilin hospital, Changchun. The collected blood samples were centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min at room temperature. The blood supernatant was stored in refrigerator at −4°C and was utilized as stock solution of serum sample for further experiments. For ALP assay, under optimized experimental conditions, aliquots of 10.0μL of 100-fold diluted human serum samples and 10.0μL of different ALP concentrations were thoroughly mixed and reacted at room temperature for 3min so as the final concentrations of ALP were in the working range of this method. Then 80.0μL of 0.01M CBS at pH 10.0 containing (1.0µM MgSO4, 0.1µM ZnCl2) and 100.0μL of 5.0mM PLP solution were added into the above premixed solution, vortex-mixed and incubated for 30min at 37°C. The resulting solution was then added to 300.0μL of diluted BSA-AuNCs with PBS at pH 7.5 (100.0μL BSA-AuNCs and 200.0μL PBS) and used for fluorimetric measurements. The recoveries of ALP in the serum samples were calculated by the aid of the regression equation between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of ALP.

Herein, we synthesized BSA-AuNCs via simple one-pot green synthetic method at the physiological temperature (37°C) using commercially available bovine serum albumin (BSA). BSA can act as both reducing and stabilizing agent for the synthesis of BSA-AuNCs and its reducing ability was enhanced after the addition of NaOH. BSA-AuNCs exhibit a characteristic red fluorescence with high quantum yield (λmax=610nm, QY ≈6%) after its excitation at 370nm as shown in Fig. 1. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images depicted in Fig. S1 shows that BSA-AuNCs were well mono-dispersed with average size of 1.95nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching of BSA-AuNCs fluorescence by PLP
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized aqueous solution of BSA-AuNCs was brown color and exhibited red fluorescence under UV lamp. Fig. 2 and S2 show the emission and absorbance spectra of BSA-AuNCs in the absence and presence of PLP, respectively. The BSA-AuNCs exhibit a strong fluorescence emission peak at about 611nm upon excitation at a wavelength of 370nm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the BSA-AuNCs exhibits a typical protein absorbance peak at about 271nm which is mainly due to the aromatic and heterocyclic residues of BSA. These features of the fluorescent emission and absorption spectra were in agreement with that in the previous report (Hu et al., 2012), indicating the successful synthesis of BSA-AuNCs.

The addition of PLP significantly quenches the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs (Fig. 2). As shown in Fig. 3, the fluorescence emission intensity of the BSA-AuNCs decreased linearly with the increase in PLP concentrations. In addition, the absorbance of BSA-AuNCs at 370nm increased in the presence of PLP (Fig. S3). In contrast, PL doesn’t quench the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs even at high concentration (1mM) (Fig. 2). As illustrated in Fig. 3, a good linear range from 50.0 μmol/ L to 1.0mmol/L is obtained for PLP detection. The Stern-Volmer equation could be used to describe the quenching in fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs by PLP according to the following: (F0/F) =1+ Ksv [Q], where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the BSA-AuNCs in the absence of PLP (excitation and emission wavelength of 370nm and 611nm, respectively) and F is the fluorescence intensity of the BSA-AuNCs in the presence of PLP, [Q] is the concentration of the PLP, and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant. Statistical analysis of the data shows good linearity with a correlation coefficient of 0.997, LOD of 6.7µmol/L at signal to noise ratio 3:1, and the limit of quantification (LOQ) of 20.0µmol/L. The Stern-Volmer constant is 5.56×10−4. The reproducibility of PLP sensing system was obtained by determining PLP (800.0µmol/L) three times with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.85%.

Reviewing the literature revealed that the quenching of AuNCs may originate from aggregation process (Dai et al., 2014; Park et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2013), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Qin et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2014) or changing the environment of BSA ligand (Chen et al., 2012; Hemmateenejad et al., 2014; Hu et al., 2012; Mathew et al., 2016). The possibility of aggregation of BSA-AuNCs by the addition of PLP has been excluded since no characteristic change in TEM or DLS (Fig. S1 and S4) and no changes in the absorbance baseline of BSA-AuNCs at wavelength (500–600nm) are observed (Fig. S3). Also, the occurrence of FRET from BSA-AuNCs to PLP was excluded owing to no overlap between the fluorescence emission of BSA-AuNCs and the absorbance spectra of PLP (Fig. 2 and Fig. S5). Here, the mechanism is proposed to be the change in the environment and the conformation of BSA template. In order to investigate the possible mechanism of PLP quenching in BSA-AuNCs fluorescence, we should confirm that the fluorescence of AuNCs is determined not only by the cluster core itself but also by the surface ligands as it is reported that surface ligands play a key role in enhancing the fluorescence of AuNCs (Wu and Jin, 2010). Xie et al. (2009) used BSA as a template for the synthesis and stabilization of AuNCs; where tyrosine residues in BSA are responsible for progressive reduction of Au3+ under alkaline conditions to form AuNCs, cysteine residues stabilizes AuNCs through covalent Au–S bond on the Au NCs surface, and BSA bulkiness provides steric protection and stabilization of AuNCs. The interaction of PLP with BSA ligand forms three types of complexes with BSA based on the previous reports by Dempsey and Christensen (1962) and Anderson et al. (1974). It is known that PLP has two main and high affinity binding sites with BSA (site I and II) with high binding constant about 106, one of which has been recognized as Lys223 or Lys220. PLP occupies site I of BSA to form an intermediate shiff base (ε-pyridoxyllysine) with the specific side chain ε-amino group of lysyl residue of BSA when added in a molar ratio 1:1 at pH 7.5 (Scheme S1). When the molar ratio increased to 2:1 at pH 7.5, the site II also occupied by PLP to form (hydrogen-bonded Schiff base product) (Scheme S1). The two previous complexes formed at site I and II seem to be highly stable and specific to PLP as the monophosphate ester group of the PLP coenzyme is necessary to form the two stable complexes at site I and II by the electrostatic attraction of the phosphate ester to a neighboring cationic site of lysine or arginine amino acids. Meanwhile, PL reacts only slightly with BSA but can’t form the characteristic stable complexes at site I and II equivalent to PLP (Dempsey and Christensen, 1962).

Fonda et al. (1991) reported another evidence for the specific role of the phosphate ester group that phosphorylated compounds and anionic compounds do not inhibit the binding of PLP to HSA, but do inhibit PLP binding to BSA. However, the exact role of the phosphate ester group of PLP is still intriguing topic. Also, they reported that PL has no significant binding with serum albumins under the same conditions of PLP reaction.

Moreover, Zhang et al. (1999) reported that the binding of PLP to BSA leads to the quenching in the fluorescence of BSA when BSA is excited at 280 (24% quenching) or 295nm (37% quenching) as PLP-BSA binding changes the microenvironment around tryptophan and tyrosine residues in BSA. Consequently, PLP interaction with BSA ligand on the surface of AuNCs could induce change in the environment of the BSA-AuNCs; hence quench the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs. So, the quenching effect of PLP could be ascribed to the specific interaction of PLP with BSA scaffold (Chen et al., 2012; Hemmateenejad et al., 2014). For further confirmation and investigation about the proposed mechanism, we performed detailed characterizations for PLP-BSA-AuNCs bioconjugate by TEM, DLS, UV–vis spectrophotometry, fluorescence spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetimes measurements and CD spectroscopy as follows:

To investigate about the quenching mechanism, TEM measurements were performed. As shown in TEM images in Fig. S1, no change in the size of BSA-AuNCs (1.95nm) in the absence and presence of 1.0mM PLP. Therefore, there is no obvious aggregation after adding PLP. This was also evidenced by DLS measurements. The hydrodynamic diameter of BSA-AuNCs is kept nearly constant even after the addition of 1.0mM PLP (Fig. S4). Consequently, aggregation induced quenching emission mechanism was ruled out.

Fig. S3 shows a significant increase in the absorbance of BSA-AuNCs at λ 370nm after the addition of 200.0µM PLP. Meanwhile, the emission spectra in Fig. 2 show that there is about 40% quenching effect in BSA-AuNCs fluorescence after the addition of 1.0mM PLP. Also, the emission peak of PLP is shifted from 450nm to 470nm upon excitation at 370nm (Fig. S2). These changes in absorbance and fluorescence spectra imply to the specific interaction between PLP and BSA ligand of BSA-AuNCs. In contrast, PL reacts negligibly with BSA and has little effect on both absorbance and emission spectra (Fig. 2).

In order to further explore the mechanism of PLP induced quenching process, fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out to efficiently distinguish mechanism of fluorescence quenching of AuNCs. The fluorescence decay of the BSA-AuNCs in the absence and presence of PLP was measured at wavelength of 626nm upon its excitation at 377nm ( Fig. 4). The fluorescence decay curves for BSA-AuNCs and PLP-BSA-AuNCs conjugate can be fitted exactly with two exponential decay functions:
I
( t )
=
I
0
+
A
1
exp
(
− t /
Γ
1
)
+
A
2
exp
(
− t /
Γ
2
)
Where I and I 0 are the fluorescence intensities at time t and 0, A 1 and A 2 are exponential constants, t is the time, and ᴦ 1 and ᴦ 2 are the fluorescence decay times for the exponential components (Wang et al., 2013). Moreover, the average fluorescence lifetimes (ᴦ) were calculated according to the equation (Wang et al., 2013):
Γ =
(
A
1
Γ
1
2
+
A
2
Γ
2
2
)
/
(
A
1
Γ
1
+
A
2
Γ
2
)
.

As shown Fig. 4, the average lifetimes are 1.805 µs and 1.761 µs for BSA-AuNCs and PLP-BSA-AuNCs conjugate, respectively. No distinct change in the average fluorescence lifetime of BSA-AuNCs after the addition of PLP indicates the absence of energy transfer processes such as FRET between BSA-AuNCs and PLP. This non-significant variation in the fluorescence lifetimes of BSA-AuNCs reveals that the static quenching is the predominant and the dynamic quenching can be excluded (Aswathy and Sony, 2014). These observations are consistent with that the interaction of PLP with BSA-AuNCs could induce conformational alteration in BSA and quench the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs.

CD spectroscopy could provide the information about structures of BSA as well as the effects of chemical modification and ligand binding. According to previous reports (Cao et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 1999, 2014, 2012), the CD spectrum of native BSA exhibited two negative minima at pH 7.4 in the UV region at 210 and 222nm which were characteristic of α-helical protein structure. While the negative band at 210nm for BSA shifted to 208nm and the valley of the band at 222nm was shallower than that of pure BSA after the synthesis of BSA-AuNCs, indicating a significant loss of α-helical structure of protein (Cao et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014, 2012). As shown in Fig. S6, the valley at 208 and 222nm became shallower in presence of PLP rather than PL, revealing that a decrease in the α-helical content and a slight increase in random coil structures of BSA-AuNCs. These results were in agreement with previous reports (Zhang et al., 1999), indicating the change in the microenvironment and conformation of BSA in presence of PLP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the developed method for the detection of ALP activity
ce:section.ce:para: ALP can hydrolyze PLP and convert it to PL which can not affect the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs, resulting in recovery of the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs quenched by PLP. It is well known that ALP activity is dependent on the type and pH of the buffer solution. Tris–HCl could not be used as it reacts with PLP and inhibits PLP-BSA binding (Fonda et al., 1991). Phosphate buffer inhibits the activity of ALP (Zhao et al., 2013). Carbonate buffer solution at pH 10.0 increases ALP activity (Delory and King, 1945; McComb and Bowers, 1972), so we selected carbonate buffer for subsequent experiments.

Fig. S7 shows that the optimum enzymatic reaction time and reaction temperature for the recovery of fluorescence intensity were 30min and 37°C, respectively. Under the optimum experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensities increase with the increased activities of ALP ( Fig. 5). A good linear calibration curve (R2 =0.995) is obtained between the relative fluorescence intensities (ΔF) and ALP activities in the range from 1.0 to 200.0 U/L (Fig. 3). The regression equation is ΔF= F0- F =0.29C +6.74; where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of BSA-AuNCs in the absence and presence of ALP. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.9% and 2.2% for seven repeated measurements of 10.0 U/L and 200.0 U/L ALP, respectively. The detection limit is 0.05 U/L calculated using the equation LOD=3Sa/b. where Sa is the standard deviation of intercept and b is the slope of the calibration curve. It is lower than the previous reported methods (Table S1), enabling us to detect ALP in biological fluids since the normal serum level of ALP in adults is about 40–190U/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the PLP quenched BSA-AuNCs for ALP assay
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of the present method towards ALP, the reaction system was treated with certain concentrations (10.0µg/L) of various enzymes, including glucose oxidase (GOx), lysozyme, trypsin, papain and pepsin under the same conditions. Also, the interference of 1.0mM of glutathione and cysteine on the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs was checked. Moreover, the effect of some ions which may exist in ALP real samples and/or play important roles for the activity of ALP was evaluated, including Mg2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, and SO4 2−. As shown in Fig. 6, all studied substances have little effect on the detection of ALP. Therefore, our method has a good selectivity for ALP in the presence of these substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP sensing in diluted human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to estimate the performance of this nanosensor probe, the developed method was applied to detect ALP in biological samples such as human serum samples. The standard addition method was utilized to measure the recoveries of this newly proposed nanosensor in diluted human serum samples. To detect recoveries, different known concentrations of ALP were spiked into the serum samples. As shown in Table S2, this proposed sensor shows reasonable recoveries in the range of 100.60–104.46% and exhibits good reproducibility with the RSDs less than 3.78%. The obtained recoveries of ALP detection are satisfactory, indicating that this proposed fluorescence switch-on strategy has good feasibility for measuring ALP in biological matrices.

PLP can quench the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs and PL cannot. Based on this different effect between PLP and PL as well as the hydrolysis of PLP to PL by ALP, we developed a facile, highly selective and sensitive “switch-on” fluorescent assay approach for sensing ALP using BSA-AuNCs. The method shows wide linear range for ALP (1–200U/L) with low detection limit of 0.05U/L. Moreover, PLP has also been detected based on its quenching effects on the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs. The proposed nanosensor was successfully applied to detect ALP in human serum samples with good recoveries in the range of 100.60–104.46%. As mentioned in introduction, ALP has broad applications and PLP acts as coenzyme for many important biological reactions, our method can open up a lot of prospect biological applications.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the kind support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475123 and 21505128), the Ministry of Science and technology of the People's Republic of China (No. 2016YFA0201300), and the Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS)–the Academy of Sciences for the Developing World (TWAS) President's Fellowship Program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.03.073.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
